coredata.dc:title: Reactive deep eutectic solvents: Refining lignocellulosic biomass of wheat straw into nano building blocks for a more sustainable sensor fabrication
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Li
Guo, Yujie
Zhou, Rui
Wu, Xiaodong
Hu, Jinguang
Huang, Mei
Shen, Fei
Tian, Dong
coredata.dc:description: Refining lignocellulosic biomass into functional building blocks in a controlled manner offers an innovative strategy and opportunity for the fabrication of biomass-based materials. However, the complex nature and recalcitrant structure of lignocellulosic biomass hinder their upgrading route. Here we propose a “deconstruction-assembly” strategy to refine low-value wheat straw into nano building blocks, specifically nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and lignin-containing cellulose nanofibers (LCNFs), for the production of versatile biomass-based sensor through a novel reactive deep eutectic solvents (DESs) refining. The DESs, composing of monoethanolamine and choline chloride, function both as solvents and reactants for lignin extraction and amination. The waste liquid fraction, containing residual aminated lignin, soluble hemicellulos, and their degradation products are used as precursor for N-CQDs preparation, while the purified cellulose is used for LCNFs production. These nano-building blocks are then employed in the fabrication of a functional temperature sensor, exhibiting high responsivity (2.26 % °C−1), linearity (R2 = 0.995), stability, and fluorescent anti-counterfeiting properties, which is highly potential for smart food package applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Temperature sensor
Carbon quantum dots
Reactive deep eutectic solvents

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of lignin-containing cellulose nanofibers (LCNFs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
Design and fabrication of CQDs-based temperature sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFE0207100), National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers: 22308235 and 42107243) and the Department of Science and Technology of Sichuan Province (grant numbers:. 2023NSFSC0321 and 2022YFN0027).

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2022YFE0207100 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers: 22308235 and 42107243 ) and the Department of Science and Technology of Sichuan Province (Grant No. 2023NSFSC0321 and 2022YFN0027 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the DESs fractionation, LCNFs/N-CQDs preparation and temperature sensor fabrication.
(a, b) FTIR spectra and (c, d) XPS survey spectra of cellulose-rich substrates and lignin samples.
(a) 2D-HSQC NMR spectra and main structures of lignin, (b) Semi-quantitative structural characterization, and (c, d) 2D-HSQC NMR and 13C NMR spectra of 2-Phenoxyethylamine.
The mechanism of MEA-ChCl DES reactive fractionation. i) selective lignin extraction/amination and ii) cellulose swelling.
TEM images of (a) LFs and (b) CQDs aqueous, and (c) Excitation-emission 3D fluorescence plots of CQDs-70, CQDs-100 and CQDs-130.

(a) Schematic diagram of CQDs/LCNFs temperature sensor structure, (b) normalized relative resistance variation to temperature, (c) comparison performances with literatures, (d) continuous measurement test, (e) repeated temperature-discrimination capacity between 16 and 34 °C, (f) relative resistance variation under bending with different curvature.

(a) CQDs-based temperature sensor applied in food package, (b) infrared thermal images of food package from refrigerator (left) and oven (right), (c) instantaneous thermal responses, (d) photographs of emitting cyan fluorescence under UV light, (e) CQDs-based ink and (f) film anti-counterfeiting on commemorative banknote under day light (left) and UV light (right).

head.ce:title: Reactive deep eutectic solvents: Refining lignocellulosic biomass of wheat straw into nano building blocks for a more sustainable sensor fabrication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sichuan Provincial Engineering Research Center of Agricultural Non-point Source Pollution Control, College of Environmental Sciences, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu, Sichuan 611130, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan Provincial Engineering Research Center of Agricultural Non-point Source Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sichuan Provincial Engineering Research Center of Agricultural Non-point Source Pollution Control, College of Environmental Sciences, Sichuan Agricultural University, Chengdu, Sichuan 611130, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Sichuan University, No.24 South Section 1, Yihuan Road, Chengdu 610065, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Sichuan University, No.24 South Section 1, Yihuan Road, Chengdu 610065, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, University of Calgary, 2500 University Dr. NW, Calgary, AB T2N 1N4, Canada

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering, University of Calgary, 2500 University Dr. NW, Calgary, AB T2N 1N4, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Refining lignocellulosic biomass into functional building blocks in a controlled manner offers an innovative strategy and opportunity for the fabrication of biomass-based materials. However, the complex nature and recalcitrant structure of lignocellulosic biomass hinder their upgrading route. Here we propose a “deconstruction-assembly” strategy to refine low-value wheat straw into nano building blocks, specifically nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) and lignin-containing cellulose nanofibers (LCNFs), for the production of versatile biomass-based sensor through a novel reactive deep eutectic solvents (DESs) refining. The DESs, composing of monoethanolamine and choline chloride, function both as solvents and reactants for lignin extraction and amination. The waste liquid fraction, containing residual aminated lignin, soluble hemicellulos, and their degradation products are used as precursor for N-CQDs preparation, while the purified cellulose is used for LCNFs production. These nano-building blocks are then employed in the fabrication of a functional temperature sensor, exhibiting high responsivity (2.26 % °C−1), linearity (R2 = 0.995), stability, and fluorescent anti-counterfeiting properties, which is highly potential for smart food package applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapidly growing demand for a sustainable bioeconomy and advancements in biomaterials technology, refining low-value and renewable biomass substrates such as lignocellulosic biomass into functional devices has received considerable attention, especially in green electronics and sensors [1,2]. The existing refining techniques can be broadly classified into two categories: the “top-down” and “bottom-up” strategies [3]. In the “top-down” approach, prevalent woody biomass with readily available micro or nano structure such as vessels, aligned cellulose nanofibers and plant cell lumens are feasible for direct fabrication [4–6], where various delignification processes have been used to preserve its natural hierarchical structure and fiber direction [7]. In addition to woody biomass, herbaceous plants such as energy crops and agriculture field wastes are the most prominent sources of lignocellulosic biomass [8]. However, the fiber morphology and lignin structural properties of herbaceous biomass differ significantly from those of woody biomass [9]. Consequently, the “top-down” approach is less feasible for low-value herbaceous biomass that exhibits lower mechanical strength and less defined nanostructures. This makes the ‘bottom-up’ construction from useful building blocks a more viable method for expanding the application of herbaceous biomass into functional devices. In the “bottom-up” strategy, platform nano building blocks can be assembled in a tailored way. Whereas hierarchically deconstructing herbaceous biomass into useful nano building blocks in a controlled manner remains a great challenge due to its complex nature and recalcitrant structure [10].

One emerging type of refining regents is deep eutectic solvents (DESs), which show high potential for lignocellulose deconstruction/fractionation owing to their selective lignin extraction capabilities. DESs are homogeneous mixtures composed of two or more components with certain ratio of hydrogen bond acceptors (HBA) and hydrogen bond donors (HBD) [11]. Thus, their properties can be easily customized via designing hydrogen bonding interactions, where biomass-derived precursors can be exploited. In our previous work, we tailored DESs composed of monoethanolamine (MEA) and chlorine chloride (ChCl), which achieved simultaneous lignin extraction and amination [12]. The in-situ lignin amination occurring during DESs extracting process could facilitate the potential high-value applications of lignin and expand the utility of DESs in the fields of lignin functional modification [13,14]. Moreover, the cleavage of hydrogen bonds between neighboring primary cellulosic fibers during alkaline DESs refining led to cellulose swelling, which enhanced nanofibrillation efficiency and facilitated the production of lignin-containing cellulose nanofibers (LCNFs) [15]. The LCNFs exhibit unique mechanical properties, optical transparency, high specific surface area, and are availability for further chemical modification. They have potential applications as biodegradable and eco-friendly templates for the development of novel sensing platforms with versatile functionality [16,17].

The applications of DESs are more than reaction media for lignocellulosic biomass hierarchical deconstruction and valorization, they can also serve as precursor for functional materials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), known for their excellent optical characteristics, high water dispersibility, and abundant surface functional groups, have great potential in photoelectric devices, fluorescent sensing and anti-counterfeiting, printing, and nano building blocks [18–20]. To date, CQDs can be prepared through many methods of chemical oxidation, laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical synthesis or hydrothermal, microwave-assisted, and pyrolysis etc., depending on whether the carbon precursors are large sp2 carbon domains or small molecules [21]. DESs, lignin and hemicellulose are known can be used as precursor to synthesize carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [22,23]. This provides the technical feasibility to produce CQDs from DESs waste liquor containing residual lignin and hemicellulose, offering a viable pathway for treating DES waste liquor and promoting sustainable utilization.

Herein, we propose a “deconstruction-assembly” strategy to transform wheat straw biomass into a versatile and flexible biomass-based temperature sensor (Fig. 1 ). The reactive DESs composed of MEA and ChCl are designed for lignocellulosic biomass hierarchical deconstruction and nano building blocks fabrication, i.e., LCNFs and N-CQDs. N-CQDs assembling around LCNFs serves as sensing layer of the temperature sensor. This facile and tailored strategy can provide insights of DESs utilization and broaden the downstream application of DESs-enabled materials. Furthermore, the flexible biomass-based sensor exhibits superior temperature-sensing performance and fluorescent anti-counterfeiting capabilities, making it a promising candidate for smart packaging applications.

Wheat straw was harvested from Sichuan Agricultural University farm, ground by a high-speed grinder and screened to 40 mesh. Dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 cyclohexanol was purchased from J&K Scientific (Beijing, China). Other reagents were purchased from Haoboyou Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China).

DESs were synthesized by MEA and ChCl in a molar ratio of 6:1 at 80 °C. 15 g wheat straw was added to 300 g DESs and continuously stirred at 70 °C, 100 °C and 130 °C for 12 h. The follow-up process was according to previous report [24]. Briefly, reaction mixture was cooled and 200 mL acetone/water mixture (1:1 by volume) was added. Cellulose-rich fraction was obtained after washing with 100 mL acetone/water mixture and 50 mL hot water. Then sulfuric acid was added to solution to adjust pH < 2 for lignin precipitation. After filtration, the resulting lignins were freeze-dried for further use, DESs waste liquor was stored for carbon quantum dots preparation. MWL was extracted [24] for comparison with DESs lignin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of lignin-containing cellulose nanofibers (LCNFs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g cellulose-rich fraction (dry weight) were suspended in 1000 mL distilled water with stirring, mechanically sheared with a household blender for 5 min and then ground by horizontal bead mill (ELE, ESW-750) at 4000 rpm for 30 min.

DESs waste liquor was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling, the mixture was washed and filtrated. The CQDs supernatant was dialyzed for 36 h using dialysis membranes with molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da, followed by filtration through a 0.2 μm membrane. Finally, purified CQDs was freeze-dried to obtain CQDs powders, which were then dispersed in distilled water (1 wt%) for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of CQDs-based temperature sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Temperature sensor was fabricated by CQDs-100 (1 mg mL−1) and LCNFs-100 (0.5 wt%) with mass ratio of 1:1 (Fig. 1). PET film was served as insulating layer to prevent moisture and heat energy dissipation. First, the conductive carbon ink (JELCON, CH-8MOD2) was silk-screen printed on insulating PET substrate, and placed on a hot plate for drying. CQDs and LCNFs were mixed and sprayed onto the sensitive region, the electrodes were covered to prevent being sprayed. Then the device was put into oven at 70 °C. At last, the sensor was covered by PET tapes to isolate it from ambient surroundings, ensuring accurate and credible measurement.

Temperature response was measured as the resistance change by a LCR meter (Tonghui, TH2817B+) and recorded by a personal computer. Ice and hot water bath were used to control the measurement temperature of sensors.

Chemical composition of the substrates was determined by Klason protocol according to TAPPI Standard Method T-222 [25]. Cellulose reactivity was determined by Simons staining (SS) technique [25]. Water retention values (WRV) was determined and calculated according to TAPPI Useful Method-256. Degree of polymerization (DP) was calculated according to its intrinsic viscosity, which was determined by dissolution in cupriethylenediamine using Ubbelohde viscometer according to ASTM D1795 [26]. Before testing, residual lignin was removed with sodium chlorite and acetic acid.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, HT7700). X-ray Diffraction pattern (XRD) was conducted on a DX-1000 diffractometer, using Cu-Ka radiation (k = 0.1540 nm) under 40 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were conducted by a Nicolet 560 spectrophotometer. 2D-HSQC spectra and 13C NMR was acquired on a Bruker AVIII 600 MHz spectrometer and was parsed by comparison with the literature using MestReNova 9 software [27,28]. Total phenolic hydroxyl group content was calculated using Folin-Ciocalteau method [29]. Elemental analysis was performed on a Vario Micro analyzer. C, H and N contents were measured, and the O content was deduced from the difference. X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectra was carried out on an XASAM 800 spectrometer. Morphology of CQDs were observed using high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (FEI Talos200X) and equipped with Bruker energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). UV–Vis spectrum was performed on an ONLAB EU-2600A spectrometer. PL spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 spectrofluorometer. The absolute fluorescence quantum yields were measured with a spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments LTD, FLS1000) equipped with an integrating sphere.

The mass balance of the overall hierarchical deconstruction of wheat straw into nano building blocks using the reactive MEA-ChCl DESs system at 70 °C, 100 °C and 130 °C was demonstrated (Fig. S1, with detailed mass balances and compositional analyses provided in Fig. S2 and Table S1). It was remarkable that MEA-ChCl DESs exhibited excellent performance in cellulose retention, corresponding to almost 100 % cellulose recovery with the assessing temperature conditions. The cellulose-rich fractions containing insoluble hemicellulose and lignin were completely converted into LCNFs through mechanical grinding. As expected, MEA-ChCl DESs significantly enriched cellulose from 34 % to ∼54 %, which was beneficial for subsequent cellulose nanofibrillation. It also showed that the employed cooking conditions could facilely remove hemicellulose and lignin through dissolution [30]. However, fractionation at higher temperature compromised lignin recovery from 76 to 50 %. After lignin regeneration, the DESs waste liquor containing soluble hemicellulose and lignin degradation products (carbon source) was further used for high-value CQDs synthesis through a hydrothermal method. Meanwhile, the remaining MEA, serving as a nitrogen source facilitated nitrogen doping in CQDs. The results indicated that lower temperature fractionation enhanced the yield of N-CQDs, with 14 % at 70 °C and 4 % at 130 °C, respectively. The possible reason was that, at milder conditions, the soluble lignin and hemicellulose underwent less extent degradation and DESs maintain original chemical structures, which was more conducive to N-CQDs synthesis. Overall, apart from an aminated lignin fraction, the reactive MEA-ChCl DES based refinery facilely converted the pristine wheat straw into available LCNFs and N-CQDs nano building blocks with 62–72 % total yields.

To understand the mechanism of reactive MEA-ChCl DESs fractionation, the morphology and chemical structure of cellulose-rich substrates and regenerated lignin were investigated in detail. The degree of polymerization (DP), water retention value (WRV) and direct orange dye adsorption (DO) capacity of the cellulose-rich substrates were determined (Table S2). The considerable DP of 792, 750 and 696 confirmed the rather stability of cellulose [31], which was attributed to the outstanding cellulose retention performance of MEA-ChCl DESs. Besides, the increased DO and WRV values indicated that the reactive DESs were more effective in improving cellulose porosity at higher temperature, due to the higher extent removal of lignin and hemicellulose. In addition to lignin and hemicellulose removal, cellulose swelling and structural deconstruction also responsible for the enhancement of cellulose porosity [24]. Owing to the up to 100 % cellulose retention and intact cellulose structure, cellulose swelling induced by the disruption of hydrogen bonds between cellulose chains layers were dominant.

As shown in Fig. 2a, the carbonyl groups (1659 cm−1) in cellulose-rich fractions probably originated from residual lignin, thereby the O–C=O peak disappeared in the spectra of Cellulose-130 with little lignin residue. Besides, the weak peak of N1s (400 eV, Fig. 2c) proved that cellulose-rich fractions contained a tiny amount of nitrogen. For the regenerated lignin (Fig. 2b), the wider absorption bands within the range of 1560–1640 cm−1 and 1030–1230 cm−1 corresponded to NH bending vibrations and CN stretching vibrations [32]. The wide peak at 3425 cm−1 was attributed to the coexistence of hydroxyl and primary amine groups [33]. Meanwhile, the apparently N1s peak (400 eV, Fig. 2d) and CN bond (286.8 eV, Fig. S4) also appeared in the spectra of regenerated lignin [34], confirming the presence of additional amine groups. The extent of amination modification of lignin was evaluated by the content of nitrogen element, increasing from 0.3 % to 1.3 %, 1.4 % and 2.0 %, respectively (Table S3). It was evident that there was a positive correlation between the reaction temperature and the amination modification extent. These results above demonstrated that amine groups were successfully grafted onto regenerated lignin during MEA-ChCl extraction process, while cellulose mainly underwent swelling.

More detailed lignin structures were characterized by a series of NMR techniques. In the 2D-HSQC spectra (Fig. 3a), all these four lignins were identified as HGS type and the lignin structures were well-preserved after DESs extraction. When the extraction temperature reached 130 °C, S condensed signal was observed. Under alkaline condition, the γ-acylated structure (A′) signals became invisible and tricin (T) signals also weakened. It was observed that the new cross peaks appeared at δC/δH 60.0–70.0/2.9–3.3 ppm and δC/δH 70.0–80.0/2.5–3.8 ppm, which were probably resulted from the introduction of amine groups [35]. In 13C NMR spectra (Fig. S5), the apparent peaks ascribed to CN related bonds were also discovered at 63 and 73 ppm in the DESs extracted lignin.

Generally, the cleavage of β–O–4′ linkages occurred during DESs extraction, leading to the increase of total phenolic hydroxyl content [36]. However, after MEA-ChCl DESs fractionation, the content of β–O–4′ linkages increased (calculated by 2D-HSQC and 13C NMR, Fig. 3b, Table S5 and S6), indicating the formation of new ether bond in MEA-ChCl extracted lignin. Meanwhile, the content of total phenolic hydroxyl reduced. This phenomenon could be explained by the fact that MEA grafted onto phenolic hydroxyl groups of lignin with ether bond, resulting in the lignin amination modification [12]. Besides, it was clearly shown that increasing the cooking temperature enhanced the extent of lignin amination, which was consistent with the results of elemental analysis. When the temperature increased to 130 °C, the resulting lignin exhibited decreased content of β–O–4′ linkages, along with the increased content of total phenolic hydroxyl groups (1.1 mmol g−1), suggesting that lignin fragmentation was dominant under sever condition. Additionally, the S/G ratios of Lignin-70 (1.0), Lignin-100 (1.0) and Lignin-130 (0.9) were lower than that of MWL (1.4), which corresponded to the reduction of -OCH3 (Table S6). This implied that the demethylation of S units occurred in MEA-ChCl DESs fractionation, resulting in a lignin composition with a higher proportion of G units.

To demonstrate the amination process analyzed above, 2-Phenoxyethylamine was used as a model compound for 13C NMR and 2D-HSQC NMR characterization (Fig. 3c and d). It was apparent that the green and red bands were assigned to α- and β- positions carbon of 2-Phenoxyethylamine. In the 13C NMR spectra of DESs lignin, the new peaks appeared at the same position (41 ppm), and strong peaks were also observed at about 70 ppm, indicating DESs lignin had similar structures to that of 2-Phenoxyethylamine. Therefore, it can be concluded that the MEA indeed reacted with lignin, forming the structures like 2-Phenoxyethylamin, which is consistent with the analysis presented above.

From the analysis above, wheat straw lignocellulose underwent two main reactions during DESs hierarchical deconstruction: lignin dissolution via the cleavage of ether bonds and cellulose swelling via the cleavage of hydrogen bonds. The MEA played an important role with simultaneous lignin extraction and amination function in this reactive DESs fractionation. When the cleavage occurred in ether bonds between lignins, the obtained phenolic hydroxyl groups would react with MEA then regenerated new ether bonds (Fig. 4i). Moreover, this process was strongly dependent on the reaction temperature. After most lignin and hemicellulose removal, cellulose fibers were exposed to DESs, resulting in the cleavages of hydrogen bonds between neighboring primary cellulosic fibers (Fig. 4ii). The cellulose swelling had limited influences on cellulose DP but could facilitate cellulose nanofibrillation.

The lignin/hemicellulose removal and cellulose swelling would facilitate the mechanical refining during LCNFs production process. Moreover, the considerable DP of DESs pretreated cellulose-rich substrates would give better mechanical properties to LCNFs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of three LCNFs were illustrated (Fig. S6, Table S7) to examine the crystal structure, which was influenced by the swelling of the crystalline cellulose portion and the removal of the amorphous lignin and hemicellulose [37]. It was clearly that the crystallinity index of LCNFs-100 was higher than of LNCFs-70, mainly due to the effective removal of amorphous lignin and hemicellulose. However, the decreased CrI value of LCNFs-130 was also observed, suggesting the swelling of crystalline cellulose portion was dominant. As shown in Fig. 5a, cellulose nanofibers and lignin nanoparticles were entangled in the network. These lignin nanoparticles in LCNFs-70 and LCNFs-100 were easier to aggregate, while those in LCNFs-130 were well-dispersed, which was closely related with the above analysis that severe lignin fragmentation occurred at higher temperature.

After lignin was regenerated from DESs, the waste liquor was subjected to hydrothermal process for CQDs synthesis. This DESs waste liquor contained residual lignin, hemicellulose and the corresponding degraded products, were common precursors for CQDs [30,38]. As shown in Fig. 5b, these three CQDs were well-dispersed and near spherical morphology with sizes below 5 nm, and the size distribution was uniform. The obvious crystal lattices proved the presence of crystalline domains. EDS analysis confirmed the CQDs were doped with nitrogen (Fig. S7). To investigate the chemical structure and composition of CQDs, FTIR and XPS spectra were analyzed. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. S8) exhibited characteristic absorption peaks, including hydroxyl groups stretching (3400 cm−1), CO stretching (1640 cm−1) and CC stretching (617 cm−1), which demonstrated that lignin fragments remaining in DESs were involved in CQDs synthesis. Additionally, the absorption bands at 1152 cm−1 (CO) was mainly originated from glycosidic bond between sugar units [22]. The typical absorption bands at 3400 cm−1, 1640 cm−1 and 1110 cm−1 were ascribed to primary amine groups stretching, NH bending and CN stretching, suggesting the CQDs were functionalized by MEA in hydrothermal process [39,40]. The XPS survey spectrum (Figs. S9–10) showed typical peak of N1s at 400 eV, which also proved the CQDs were nitrogen-doped. The high-resolution spectra of N1s revealed peaks at 399.7 and 402.1 eV, corresponding to NH and graphitic N [41]. In addition, the high-resolution spectra of C1s and O1s proved the presence of CO, CO, CN and OH, which was consistent with the results of FTIR characterization.

The optical properties of CQDs were further studied by UV–vis absorption spectra and excitation-emission 3D fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S11). The well-defined characteristic band at 280 nm was related to phenylpropane units of lignin fragments, while the weak characteristic peak at 229 nm was assigned to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bond [22].The strong emission and excitation zoom were located at approximately 425 and 227 nm (Fig. 5c), indicating a large Stokes shift of 198 nm, which reduced the photoluminescence (PL) interference from excitation light. In addition, another two weak excitation peaks were also observed especially in CQDs-70. These peaks were mainly attributed to a transition in σ and π orbitals from HOMO to LUMO [42]. Nevertheless, the emission maxima maintained the same position within the excitation range between 200 and 250 nm, which is characteristic of excitation-independent of organic fluorophores [43]. When increasing excitation wavelength from 250 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence peaks apparently red shifted and the PL intensity sharply decreased, associating with surface- or defect states in the amorphous carbon shell of CQDs. The PL quantum yields (QYs) of CQDs-70, CQDs-100 and CQDs-130 were 7.9 %, 7.2 % and 6.0 %, respectively, which were comparable to results reported by other works [39,44]. Generally, surface functionalization could stabilize the surface defect sites to promote the radiative recombination process, resulting in an increase of fluorescence QY [45]. Thus, the QYs of these three CQDs were consistent with their nitrogen content obtained by EDS and XPS results.

The LCNFs-100 and CQDs-100 with considerable higher yields and better properties were further used for the temperature sensor fabrication (Fig. 6a). The sensing layer was composed of luminescent CQDs self-assembled around LCNFs with mass ratio of 1:1. PET film and tape were respectively served as substrate and cover layer, to protect the temperature sensor from moisture and heat energy dissipation, ensuring measurement accuracy. As illustrated in Fig. 6b, the CQDs-based temperature sensor exhibited a negative temperature coefficient (NTC) behavior in the temperature range of 10 to 40 °C, which was attributed to thermal-excitation resulting in an increasing mobilities of electron, hole pairs and residual ion from DESs [46]. Meanwhile, the linear curve fitting showed good linearity between ΔR/R0 and temperatures (R2 = 0.995). Generally, temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) was used to evaluate the thermal sensitivity, which was calculated as TCR = [(R- R0)/R0)]/ΔT [47]. It was observed that the CQDs/LCNFs sensor displayed a high TCR value of −0.0226 °C -1, which was higher than that of prevalent temperature sensors. Compared to other temperature sensors summarized in Fig. 6c, the CQDs/LCNFs temperature sensor achieved extremely high sensitivity and specific low temperature sensing range, which showed considerable potential for highly sensitive electrical temperature-measurements even under cold condition [48–55].

Fig. 6d showed that the continuous resistance signals were almost static, suggesting the stable temperature-sensing capability of CQDs/LCNFs sensor. Moreover, this sensor revealed high repeatability and discrimination capacity of temperature-sensing, as evidenced by the negligible deviation of ΔR/R0 during repetitive temperature cycles (Fig. 6e). When applied in the field of smart packaging, they would inevitably deform during transportation and employ process. Therefore, it was essential for the sensor to maintain its temperature-sensing performance under such deformation. Herein, the CQDs/LCNFs sensor was bent with different curvatures (Fig. 6f). As expected, the bending had quite limited influence on the temperature-sensing performance of the CQDs/LCNFs sensor.

As shown in Fig. 7a, the CQDs/LCNFs temperature sensor were printed on the package of fresh vegetable. The package with temperature sensor was respectively put in the refrigerator and oven to assessing its sensing ability. An infrared (IR) thermography camera was used to determine the instantaneous temperature (Fig. 7b). Simultaneously, continuous resistance variation was measured by the CQDs/LCNFs temperature sensor (Fig. 7c). This sensor displayed the ΔR/R0 about 0.22, which was consistent with the temperature in cold condition (14 °C). Whereas the response ΔR/R0 sharply decreased to about −0.16 when the temperature rose to 29 °C. The consistency between sensor-measured temperature and infrared thermal image proved reliable thermosensitive response in practical application. In addition to temperature-sensing, the CQDs-based sensor also could serve as a security label for fluorescent anti-counterfeiting. It was observed that this sensor fluoresced distinctly under UV light (Fig. 7d). To evaluate the performance of the CQDs as anti-counterfeiting ink and film, the CQDs-based composite ink and film were dripped on and posted on commemorative banknotes, respectively. Fig. 7e and f showed that the mark was more inconspicuous than the film under daylight. When UV lamp was irradiated on the commemorative banknotes, a cyan-emitted mark and film appeared. Whereas the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-based composite ink and film was weaker than that of the standard anti-counterfeiting label (the yellow character “20”).

In this work, we reported a “deconstruction-assembly” strategy to transform wheat straw biomass into a versatile biomass-based temperature sensor with excellent thermal response and fluorescent anti-counterfeiting, making it a promising candidate for smart food packaging. The reactive DESs composed of MEA and ChCl showed dual function of lignocellulose fractionation and N doping, providing the technical feasibility for nano building blocks fabrication. The fractionation process using reactive DESs had two main stages: i) Lignin dissolution through β–O–4′ cleavage, accompanied by simultaneous lignin amination between MEA and lignin phenolic hydroxyl groups. ii) Cellulose swelling due to the cleavages of hydrogen bonds between interconnecting neighboring primary cellulose fibers. In addition to lignin extraction and amination, MEA was also involved in N-CQDs synthesis as nitrogen source. In conclusion, this work paved a new technical way of refining straw biomass into nano building blocks platform that could be further flexibly assembled to functional devices. This strategy also offers a promising avenue for the valorization of other complex and low-value biomass materials.

Li Zhao: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yujie Guo: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Rui Zhou: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaodong Wu: Validation, Data curation. Jinguang Hu: Visualization, Validation. Mei Huang: Conceptualization. Fei Shen: Supervision, Conceptualization. Dong Tian: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFE0207100), National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers: 22308235 and 42107243) and the Department of Science and Technology of Sichuan Province (grant numbers:. 2023NSFSC0321 and 2022YFN0027).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.142282.
